best_guess_labels: things in the bible that have been found

evidence.page_title: Was This Bible Found at the Bottom of the Ocean? | Snopes.com
evidence.title: Was This Bible Found at the Bottom of the Ocean?
evidence.description: The intact recovery of seemingly fragile objects from the ocean's depths has long fascinated the public imagination.
evidence.text:
Claims of Bibles being found undamaged and intact at the site of disasters (e.g., fires, shipwrecks) that destroyed nearly everything else around them is a common religious trope, one often likened to a modern-day form of miracle.
One putative instance of that phenomenon which is frequently shared on social media is a photograph of an encrusted book described as an "ancient Bible" that was "found at the bottom of the ocean" and yet remained "still readable":
In fact, the pictured object was not a Bible, nor was it discovered in the depths of any ocean. The tome was actually a German-English dictionary used as part of a crystallization experiment described and pictured in a 2014 blog post.
The experimenter, Catherine McEver, described on her "Stuff You Can't Have" blog how seeing crystallized books during a maker studio tour in San Francisco inspired her to engage in some artistic crystallisation experiments of her own using books and other paper ephemera:
Interest in crystallization was piqued by a maker studio tour in San Francisco, during which I saw artist Alexis Arnold's crystallized books made using 20 Mule Team Borax. They were enchanting and I wanted to know how it was done, but she was charging $400 for a how-to workshop. Upon googling after arriving home I found that the 20 Mule Team Borax website offers the dead-simple instructions for growing crystals absolutely free, and a box of Borax from the local supermarket costs less than $6.
The formula:
- Use a glass or porcelain container large enough to immerse whatever object you want to crystallize.
- Boil water, and make a super saturated solution in your container using 1 cup boiled water per 3 tablespoons Borax.
- Immerse object (and in the case of the book, arrange pages using chopsticks) and wait for crystals to grow.
- When you think it's crystallized enough, remove from solution and dry on drying rack. The book shown took less than 24 hours.The end result is a fixed object - crystallized and surprisingly heavy.
McEver performed similar crystallization experiments on objects such as reading glasses, photographs, seashells and rocks, and even fairy dresses.

evidence.page_title: Crystallised old dictionary, not 'bible found at bottom of ocean'
evidence.title: Crystallised old dictionary, not ‘bible found at bottom of ocean’
evidence.description: A photo of a book covered in white crystals has been posted on Facebook with a claim that it shows a miracle.The caption reads: “An ancient bible has been fou...
evidence.text:
A photo of a book covered in white crystals has been posted on Facebook with a claim that it shows a miracle.
The caption reads: “An ancient bible has been found at the bottom of the ocean and is still Readable! Type Amen and share this Miracle.”
It was shared on a Kenyan Facebook group page with more than 140,000 members. Many users who saw it seemed convinced of the “miracle”, and typed “Amen”.
But a reverse image search shows that the image first appeared online on 18 May 2014 in a blog article about artistic crystallisation experiments on books and paper.
We contacted the author, Catherine McEver, who is based in the US state of California.
“I am the artist who created the crystallised book you are referring to,” she told Africa Check. “It was made as a piece of art and has nothing to do with the bible or religion.”
McEver said she was aware that people had been using her photo to spread false information.
“I became aware that someone stole the crystallised book image from my blog a while ago and was falsely claiming it was an ancient bible fished out of the sea. That is not true. It was art, made by me, just a few years ago.”
She said the book is “an old German-English dictionary” – not a bible.
She also explained how she gave the book its appearance.
“The process of crystallising the book is quite easy. You make a super-saturated solution of 20 Mule Team Borax and then immerse the book (or any object) in the solution until crystals form.
“You will find complete instructions as well as many other types of objects I have crystallised on my blog.” - Grace Gichuhi (11/06/2019)
The caption reads: “An ancient bible has been found at the bottom of the ocean and is still Readable! Type Amen and share this Miracle.”
It was shared on a Kenyan Facebook group page with more than 140,000 members. Many users who saw it seemed convinced of the “miracle”, and typed “Amen”.
But a reverse image search shows that the image first appeared online on 18 May 2014 in a blog article about artistic crystallisation experiments on books and paper.
We contacted the author, Catherine McEver, who is based in the US state of California.
“I am the artist who created the crystallised book you are referring to,” she told Africa Check. “It was made as a piece of art and has nothing to do with the bible or religion.”
Old German-English dictionary
McEver said she was aware that people had been using her photo to spread false information.
“I became aware that someone stole the crystallised book image from my blog a while ago and was falsely claiming it was an ancient bible fished out of the sea. That is not true. It was art, made by me, just a few years ago.”
She said the book is “an old German-English dictionary” – not a bible.
She also explained how she gave the book its appearance.
“The process of crystallising the book is quite easy. You make a super-saturated solution of 20 Mule Team Borax and then immerse the book (or any object) in the solution until crystals form.
“You will find complete instructions as well as many other types of objects I have crystallised on my blog.” - Grace Gichuhi (11/06/2019)
For publishers: what to do if your post is rated false
A fact-checker has rated your Facebook or Instagram post as “false”, “altered”, “partly false” or “missing context”. This could have serious consequences. What do you do?
Click on our guide for the steps you should follow.
Publishers guideAfrica Check teams up with Facebook
Africa Check is a partner in Meta's third-party fact-checking programme to help stop the spread of false information on social media.
The content we rate as “false” will be downgraded on Facebook and Instagram. This means fewer people will see it.
You can also help identify false information on Facebook. This guide explains how.
image_captions: A screenshot of Kenyan president William Ruto in a church in Karen, Nairobi in April 2026. Screengrab/STATE HOUSE

evidence.page_title: Crystallization Experiments 1: Books and Paper Ephemera
evidence.title: Crystallization Experiments 1: Books and Paper Ephemera
evidence.description: Crystallized German-English Dictionary Interest in crystallization was piqued by a maker studio tour in San Francisco, during which...
evidence.text:
Sunday, May 18, 2014
Crystallization Experiments 1: Books and Paper Ephemera
65 comments:
- AnonymousMay 20, 2014 at 1:10 AM
This is amazing - what surreal effects on everyday objects. I will look forward to seeing what you do.
ReplyDelete Have been trying this. How did you get a large enough glass container and did you suspend it or just lay it flat. Also, did you cover the mixture?
ReplyDeleteRepliesUse a large ceramic bowl - visit a local thrift store and you can pick one up cheaply. Hmmm...come to think I'd it, a small used fish tank would really work well. Note with the box above that instead of lying flat it looks like the it is slightly closed. That is because it is following the curve of a large ceramic bowl I used.
Delete
For books, just lay it in the bowl. Use chopsticks or another implement to artfully arrange the pages once it is immersed. For a small fairy dress I crystallized I did suspend it in a mason jar. Just use your ingenuity and creativity and experiment.
And NO, do not cover it. The process of crystallization depends on evaporation.
Hope this helped.Rats..spell correction on my iPad changed some of the words above. That's "book," not "box."
Delete
- AnonymousDecember 22, 2016 at 4:39 PM
How long was it submerged? Also how long did it take to dry? I know you said total time was less than 24hours but if you remember those time that would be helpful. Thank you!
ReplyDeleteRepliesHmmm...this was from two years ago so your guess is as good as mine. Also, the shape and density of the object you are crystallizing as well as the climate of the place you're working would affect this. Experiment, like I did. Keep an eye on it as it crystallizes, pull it when you think it is crystallized enough, and then it will take however long it takes to dry. How long do you think a soaking wet book would take to dry?
Delete- AnonymousFebruary 7, 2024 at 12:04 AM
Even after two years, your “guess” would still be better than theirs, since you’re the one who did it. What a weirdly dismissive thing to say.
Delete - AnonymousFebruary 7, 2024 at 9:30 AM
Sorry, I was just being honest about not remembering. My work and my blog is all about experimenting and encouraging others to do the same. Each object crystallizes differently. I do NOT have all the answers, I’m just sharing experiments.
Delete
- Replies
I have no idea, I am experimenting myself and encourage you to do the same. If you look at my crystallization posts you will see it worked very well on seashells, so I imagine it would work with bone also. I never coat mine with anything, but that is another interesting experiment and may help prevent oxidization (slow whitening of the crystals) down the road.
Delete
- CourtneyApril 15, 2017 at 6:22 PM
Do the crystals stay on, or fall out when touched after dry?
ReplyDelete - AnonymousApril 19, 2017 at 1:01 PM
I was wondering if I had crystalized the book covers of a sketchbook, if i would be able to open the book still? I want to do it before so I don't mess up my class project!
ReplyDeleteRepliesMy knee jerk reaction would be no - crystal won't flex and bend like a leather or paper cover. On the other hand, if the front and back cover pieces were separate, individual pieces, and you were going to rebound the whole thing with larger than normal spiral binding, it just might work.
Delete
hey! i'd like to do this to a photo. do you think the photographic paper is too smooth/ slippery so the crystalls might just fall off?
ReplyDeleteThe chopsticks are just an implement. I used them to turn a page under, shove pages together a bit more, separate pages slightly - whatever is needed. And you use them the way you always use chopsticks: just hold them in your hand and use them to grasp or manipulate things. I don't leave them in the bowl. If I did, they'd grow crystals themselves. So no, they don't get stuck to the book. And because the bowl is glass the book doesn't stick to the bowl.
ReplyDeleteIs there any reason why I could be getting small thin crystals on the surface instead of big chunky ones? I am doing everything right it seems and have been waiting like 15 hours by now. Also, does the book need to float in the solution aka does the bowl need to be filled to the absolute top?
ReplyDeleteUpdate: I was reading more about this and noticed one person made it seem like the crystals don't fully develop until out of the water and drying. Is this correct?
ReplyDeleteRepliesAll I can advise is experimentation - I'm not a crystal expert. But no, the book doesn't need to float and probably couldn't, given physics and all. And no, in my experience crystals form while the object is in the crystal solution, not when out of the water. Are you moving the book or agitating the water? That could slow/prevent crystal growth. Is your book or object made of natural materials? Crystals don't grow well on plastic or synthetics. Other than that, just keep trying. Good luck!
Delete
- AnonymousMay 20, 2020 at 6:43 AM
Will the container get cover in crystals too? Will crystals grow on the sides and bottom of the container. If I use a ceramic cooking pot then after i clean it out can i still cook with it.
ReplyDelete Ho fatto un tentativo lo scorso anno ed il libro era venuto benissimo, però dopo un anno adesso è diventato tutta polvere😩. Cosa di potrebbe fare per evitare questo?
ReplyDeleteDo you put the book in while the water is still boiling or do you wait for it stop boiling then put it in?
ReplyDeleteRepliesHmm, you’re right, that isn’t clear in my directions. Once you have booked the borax and water and have created a totally dissolved, super-saturated solution (and this can be done in any pot), you THEN pour that solution into a ceramic or enameled or glass container. You can carefully arrange the object in the container and gently add the solution, or you can use a tall vertical container and figure out how to suspend the object. Chopsticks are useful to tweak your arrangement while the solution is still hot. Then leave it alone!
Delete- AnonymousMarch 2, 2023 at 7:28 PM
I put my book in the water and it immediately fell apart. Is my water to hot?
Delete
- AnonymousMay 3, 2022 at 9:21 PM
So I know whether to be carful or not bc I’ve made crystals before this way but can’t remember and have never made something this big. Once all down and dried off there isn’t any reason why it can’t come in contact with liquids right like if I spill water by accident it should be fine
ReplyDelete - AnonymousJuly 1, 2022 at 10:57 AM
Plastic will grow crystals itself, so ceramic or glass is best. But depending on what you’re doing you may get away with it - just be prepared to have to yank/pry/hack your crystallized object away from the crystals that are growing on sides and bottom of bucket. My rule of thumb is experiment, experiment, experiment.
ReplyDelete - AnonymousFebruary 5, 2023 at 3:31 PM
Can you put dye in it to make the crystals colorful?
ReplyDelete - AnonymousMarch 14, 2023 at 12:44 PM
I’m really confused can you send me step by step instructions. I’ve boiled the water then added the borax to the boiling water I then added the book I then transferred it into a container. It looks like it’s not doing anything
ReplyDeleteReplies- AnonymousOctober 24, 2023 at 11:05 AM
Catherine McEver responding to March 14, 2023 comment above: You have to leave the book in the container with the super solution of water and borax - for as long as 24 hours, or until you see crystals form and they reach the amount of crystallization you want. Then remove it. You don't just dip it in the Borax solution!
Delete
- AnonymousOctober 22, 2023 at 8:05 PM
I'm having an issues with my books turning white(the crystals) after it dries. How do I stop that?
ReplyDelete - AnonymousOctober 24, 2023 at 11:02 AM
Hi- Catherine McEver here responding to the above (Blogger won't let me comment under "reply"). I have had the "crystals turning white" issue also, and this has come up before in this comment stream. Some objects turn, others don't, and I don't know why. An earlier commenter suggested sealing the piece somehow. I haven't tried that, but I encourage experimentation!
ReplyDelete - AnonymousOctober 29, 2023 at 2:29 AM
Hi can I reuse the solution again once I’ve taken the book out?
ReplyDelete - AnonymousOctober 30, 2023 at 11:45 AM
Re can I reuse solution again? No, you would have to reboil it to recreate a super-saturated solution first.
ReplyDeleteReplies- AnonymousMay 1, 2025 at 6:53 AM
To clarify: can I reboil the same solution? It's got some crystals formed in it but I'm guessing they'd dissipate on boiling. Wondering if I'd have to add more borax or if reboiling will let me do more with the original solution (and for how long/how many times, I guess). BTW this is all so beautiful and I'm having a blast trying it out!
Delete
- AnonymousOctober 30, 2023 at 11:46 AM
Re adding food coloring: I've never tried it, but can't think why it wouldn't work. Experiment and report back!
ReplyDelete - AnonymousNovember 11, 2023 at 9:29 AM
I have cats will it effect them if per chance they lick it ??
ReplyDelete - AnonymousDecember 11, 2023 at 12:41 PM
I just tried my first book and I’m pretty happy with the results. I’m definitely going to try more.
ReplyDelete - AnonymousDecember 29, 2023 at 6:04 PM
I crystallized two books & I have ppl online that asked to buy. Does anyone know what I could put on the book once it’s done to seal the crystals in place so I could ship it off if someone were to purchase it online? I gifted a book and some of the crystals fell off when picked up. There has to be something u can spray on it to keep them in place and not fall off.
ReplyDelete - AnonymousDecember 29, 2023 at 6:07 PM
What can u seal the book with so the crystals do not come off? I have done a couple books and I have ppl that want to buy them. So if I’m shipping the book I don’t want the crystals to come off when it’s being shipped off. (UPS and fedex are rough with the packages sometimes) there has to be something to seal it with that way the crystals don’t come off. Any suggestions?
ReplyDelete Can anyone answer how you’ve gotten your books to stay together in the boiling water? My husband planned a day date and this was the adventure we went on. Bought the book, supplies etc. thankfully I said I wanted to do a test run on a spare book I had. It completely fell apart when it went into the water. I plan to to try again but just looking for some advice before I make another attempt. TIA!!
ReplyDelete- AnonymousApril 11, 2024 at 3:56 PM
Can you move the bucket from one place to another while the crystal is forming?
ReplyDelete - AnonymousAugust 25, 2024 at 2:31 PM
First , I want to thank you for offering the steps to making this beautiful crystallized book! I have been looking for some different type of crystal creations , due to the fact , that I always want to create things that are beautiful, difficult to accomplish and that most people either don’t know about , or maybe do but don’t want to diy it ! Also that people don’t own . I mean so many are creating borax crystals on stuff , but are they all creating cool looking artsy crystal books?? .. plus I am soooo picky ! So Your book came out beautifully and can’t wait to give this one a try!! ❤️❤️❤️
ReplyDelete - AnonymousFebruary 2, 2026 at 8:38 AM
I emerged cotton and wool textile pieces into a borax solution, which gave a beautiful crystallised result. Unfortunately, after the objects were exposed to natural and artificial light, the crystals became mat and white. Can I resolve this = making the crystals shinny again, when I dip the textile objects for a short while again in a borax solution? Many thanks!
ReplyDelete
